---
title: "Likelihoods, Priors, and MAP Estimation"
description: "A bridge page showing how observation models, priors, and posterior modes turn inference questions into optimization problems."
page-type: application
status: published
update-status: current
level: bridge
topic: optimization-and-inference
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/statistics/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Interpret a likelihood as a data-fit model rather than just a formula."
  - "Interpret a prior or regularizer as a structural assumption about hidden quantities."
  - "Explain how MAP estimation turns posterior inference into an optimization problem."
  - "Recognize common correspondences between noise models, priors, and objective functions."
applications:
  - inference
  - optimization
  - estimation
  - bayesian
keywords:
  - likelihood
  - prior
  - MAP
  - regularization
  - inference
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-26
source-check-date: 2026-04-26
review-cadence: semiannual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
---

## Application Snapshot

Once an inference problem has observations and a hidden target, the next question is:

`how should evidence from the data be combined with assumptions about what hidden solutions are plausible?`

The two main pieces are:

- a `likelihood`, which scores how well a hidden candidate explains the data
- a `prior`, which encodes which hidden candidates look more plausible before seeing the data

`MAP estimation` is the bridge that turns those two pieces into a concrete optimization problem.

## Problem Setting

Suppose the hidden quantity is $x$ and the observed data are $y$.

The likelihood is the model

$$
p(y \mid x),
$$

which says how probable the observations would be if $x$ were the truth.

The prior is

$$
p(x),
$$

which says which values of $x$ are more plausible before observing $y$.

Bayes' rule combines them:

$$
p(x \mid y)
\propto
p(y \mid x)\,p(x).
$$

If you want a single best posterior mode instead of the whole posterior distribution, you get the MAP estimator:

$$
\hat{x}_{\mathrm{MAP}}
=
\arg\max_x p(x \mid y)
=
\arg\max_x p(y \mid x)p(x).
$$

Taking negative logs turns that into an optimization problem:

$$
\hat{x}_{\mathrm{MAP}}
=
\arg\min_x \bigl[-\log p(y \mid x) - \log p(x)\bigr].
$$

## Why This Math Appears

This page sits exactly at the intersection of several site modules:

- `Statistics`: likelihoods, posteriors, Bayesian estimation
- `Optimization`: objectives, constraints, convexity, regularization
- `High-Dimensional Statistics`: sparsity assumptions and structured recovery
- `Signal Processing and Estimation`: noisy measurements and inverse problems
- `Information Theory`: priors and penalties as ways of controlling uncertainty and description complexity

So MAP estimation is not a niche Bayesian trick. It is one of the cleanest ways to translate probabilistic modeling into a numerical objective you can actually solve.

## Math Objects In Use

- hidden variable, parameter, or signal $x$
- observation $y$
- likelihood $p(y \mid x)$
- prior $p(x)$
- posterior $p(x \mid y)$
- negative log-likelihood as a data-fit term
- negative log-prior as a penalty or regularizer

This is why the same optimization template appears across many applications:

$$
\min_x \Bigl[\text{data fit}(x) + \lambda\,\text{regularizer}(x)\Bigr].
$$

In many cases, the regularizer is just the negative log-prior up to constants and scaling.

## A Small Worked Walkthrough

Take the simple noisy observation model

$$
y = x + \eta,
\qquad
\eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2).
$$

Then the likelihood says:

$$
p(y \mid x) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y-x)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right).
$$

Now suppose the prior is also Gaussian:

$$
x \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\tau^2).
$$

Then

$$
p(x) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\tau^2}\right).
$$

The MAP problem becomes

$$
\hat{x}_{\mathrm{MAP}}
=
\arg\min_x
\left[
\frac{(y-x)^2}{2\sigma^2}

+ \frac{x^2}{2\tau^2}
\right].
$$

This is already the shape of a regularized objective:

- the first term fits the data
- the second term shrinks solutions toward values preferred by the prior

If the prior were Laplace instead of Gaussian, the penalty would become proportional to $|x|$, which is the same geometry that later reappears in sparse recovery and `l1` regularization.

So one of the most important application translations is:

- Gaussian noise often leads to squared loss
- Gaussian priors often lead to `l2` penalties
- Laplace priors often lead to `l1` penalties

## Implementation or Computation Note

In practice, three decisions matter immediately:

1. `Model choice`
   What noise model makes sense for the measurement process?

2. `Structure choice`
   What prior or regularizer expresses what you believe about the hidden quantity?

3. `Computation choice`
   Is the resulting objective easy to optimize, or will you need approximation or sampling?

The strongest next pages after this one are:

- [Filtering, Smoothing, and Hidden-State Inference](filtering-smoothing-and-hidden-state-inference.qmd)
- [Numerical Least Squares and Regularization](../../topics/advanced/numerical-methods/concepts/numerical-least-squares-and-regularization.qmd)
- [Lasso and Compressed Sensing Basics](../../topics/advanced/high-dimensional-statistics/concepts/lasso-and-compressed-sensing-basics.qmd)
- [Variational Objectives, ELBO, and Information Bounds](../../topics/advanced/information-theory/concepts/variational-objectives-elbo-and-information-bounds.qmd)
- [Mixing, Ergodicity, and MCMC Bridges](../../topics/advanced/stochastic-processes/concepts/mixing-ergodicity-and-mcmc-bridges.qmd)

## Failure Modes

- confusing `MLE`, `MAP`, and full Bayesian posterior inference
- treating the prior as decoration instead of a real structural assumption
- forgetting that a bad likelihood model can dominate everything downstream
- interpreting the MAP point estimate as if it contained the same information as the whole posterior
- tuning penalties numerically without asking what prior belief or recovery bias they actually encode

## Paper Bridge

- [STATS 305B / Applied Statistics II](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats305b/) - `First pass` - useful once regularization and posterior-mode estimation start to merge. Checked `2026-04-26`.
- [EE278 / Introduction to Statistical Signal Processing](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee278/) - `Bridge to estimation` - useful once likelihood modeling and noisy observations become the main bottleneck. Checked `2026-04-26`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [STATS 202 / Data Mining and Analysis](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats202/) - `First pass` - official Stanford bridge for estimation viewpoints and modeling choices. Checked `2026-04-26`.
- [STATS 305B / Applied Statistics II](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats305b/) - `First pass` - official Stanford anchor for regularization and modern estimation language. Checked `2026-04-26`.
- [STATS 305B LASSO Notes](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats305b/notes/LASSO_I.html) - `Second pass` - official Stanford notes that make the penalty-as-structure viewpoint explicit. Checked `2026-04-26`.
- [6.011 / Signals, Systems and Inference](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-011-signals-systems-and-inference-spring-2018/) - `Bridge to noisy measurements` - official MIT course anchor for inference from corrupted observations. Checked `2026-04-26`.
- [16.322 / Stochastic Estimation and Control](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/16-322-stochastic-estimation-and-control-fall-2004/) - `Bridge to model-based estimation` - official MIT source for hidden-state estimation and Bayesian filtering viewpoints. Checked `2026-04-26`.
